segments.text: of the bill that are being presented to broaden the funding
from energy related to charitable purposes.
And today, the trust can only fund energy related purposes or
projects. This bill would enable the trust to support any charitable purpose that
benefits the district community, in addition to continuing to support energy
related purposes. And I think
it's worth noting that they would then have to
determine what their eligibility criteria looks like, and what their vision
for the region was, and how their grant funding would
help to support that.
And the second purpose, the bill will alter the trust deed so it can be updated in
the future with safeguards, which allows for future flexibility.
Instead of needing another act of Parliament for future trust deed updates, the
bill allows future amendments with strong safeguards, and trustees would be able to
amend terms only where it would be in the trust's best interest to
further the charitable purpose.
I think it's worth understanding a little bit about the Eastern Bay of Plenty
community.
Population size, 37,000.
Māori, the share of the Māori population there, 56%, large
Māori community. High area of
deprivation. Average household income, well below the New Zealand
average. The benefits received, very high in terms of comparison
with the New Zealand average. Home ownership, low.
Rent affordability pressure, very high.
NCEA level two at age 18, well below average.
And the NEATS rate, very high. School leavers with no qualification, well
above the national average. The community is in
need of support in ways that it can
deliver better projects and better community deliverables
in so many different areas in the voluntary and community organization space.
In the Eastern Bay of Plenty, there's only a certain number of energy related
purposes available to support within the district, which has led to 30 years of
static grant distribution despite population growth.
By the end of this year, the trust will have distributed about $55
million in energy related purposes, and the trust estimates this
change could mean an additional 1 million to 2 million more per year available for
the community in the Eastern Bay of Plenty, which supports community and sporting
organizations, and the many volunteers who keep the public sector and the
not-for-profit organizations going.
At the same time, as I said, the community wellbeing
goes well beyond energy, and the limitation on making grants only for energy
related purposes restricts the trust's ability to respond to changing community
needs or to support charities, societies, and other worthy groups that
do not meet this definition. With the present limitation on energy related
purposes, the trust has been able to grant about $900 per person in its
district over the past 30 years. And by contrast, the neighboring
Rototuna Trust, which has broader charitable purposes, has been
able to grant about $2,000 per person in its district over the same
period. In considering the need for change, the trustees have traversed many
conversations, and I think it worth covering off some of those for the benefit
today. As was statutorily required, the trust consulted widely,
including conducting a poll, of which 3,625 people
responded to, and 80.7% of those supported the changes.
Those who supported the bill supported a wider range of social, educational,
cultural, and environmental initiatives to include a wider variety of community
projects and groups. This expansion is seen as a way to strengthen
healthy, vibrant, and innovative communities, by providing grants that address
diverse needs. For example, youth initiatives, education, wellbeing,
community partnerships, and support for Māori and rural communities were frequently
highlighted as important areas for funding.
Of those who opposed the changes, many felt it might result in higher power prices
for consumers, but this often comes from confusion about the difference between the
trust and the electricity distribution company, Horizon Energy.
Related to this, the trustees considered a variation of the energy related purposes
under Part 3 of the Charitable Trusts Act, so they looked to see if that was
an easier way to make this change. However, the ability to
vary charitable purposes under the Charitable Trusts Act
is limited, and the trustees do not want a narrow change to the energy
related purposes. They desire broader charitable purposes, which they can then
define through their funding criteria and eligibility, to allow them to do
more and fund more in the community.
The trustees explored a consumer distribution model, but Trust Horizon is quite
different to most other energy trusts in that it is a charitable trust,
so in distributing funds, it has to meet both the trustee purpose and be
charitable in law. Providing a small, regular payment to all electricity
users, as some consumer trusts do around the country,
is not a charitable activity and so would not qualify.
The trust instead granted up to $2,000 to support charitable causes with
energy bills and co-funded the EECA Warmer Kiwi Homes initiatives to make
insulation and heating free for anyone that was eligible.
This equated to, on average, $1,000 for insulation and
$700 for heating. The trust has been
integral in insulating more than 50% of all homes in the district through its
insulation contribution, creating energy savings and healthy homes for past,
current, and future generations.
And it has a community energy contribution grant each year for up to
$2,500 per eligible organization, to
assist with the impact of power prices.
It provides low interest lending for organizations wanting to install solar and has
deployed over $1 million in funding to date for this purpose.
This includes lots of different equipment, such as electric lawnmowers,
rollers, electric mobility vans, school EV
chargers, community bike chargers, and others.
It supports local groups with the energy transition funding projects at around 1 to
$2 million in granting a year. And still, the funds accumulate due to the narrow
nature of the trust. So the trustees have agreed that a wider charitable
purpose would be the most efficient way for the trust to assist with that need, and
a private bill is the only way to achieve that goal.

groups across Whakatane, Kawerau, Opotiki, and Kaingaroa Village.
In their words, the long-term vision is a thriving Eastern Bay, where our funding
can respond to what communities need most over time, and where the trust can invest
for durable outcomes rather than being locked into a narrow 1990s
definition of energy-related purposes.

At its core, this bill is about enabling Trust Horizon to continue
fulfilling its charitable mission of providing transformational
investing into our community well into the future
by unlocking funding beyond energy-related purposes to ensure
our governing deed reflects and is responsive to current and future
community needs.
Trust Horizon was established in '94 as part of the electricity
sector reforms under the Energy Companies Act.
Our primary purpose is to grant funds back into the local communities,
with over 50 million granted over our 30 plus years of operation.
One of our proudest achievements has been early and sustained investment
into home insulation, alongside the government, helping
to insulate tens of thousands of homes, reduce energy poverty, and improve
public health outcomes.
The trust now holds assets of over 200 million, making it a
significant and enduring philanthropic
institution in one of New Zealand's most socioeconomically
deprived areas.
The trust has a vision of enabling pride, prosperity, and people through our
funding.
In order to be able to make grants to the community, we hold a range of
investments,
our largest investment being in the Horizon Energy Group, which is an
independently governed and run company that evolved from the original
setter of the trust.
Despite our scale and capacity, Trust Horizon faces a fundamental constraint.
Our trust deed limits us to funding energy-related purposes only.
Trust Horizon is now the only energy trust in New Zealand that remains
exclusively restricted in this way.
That's right.
While energy remains critical, especially for households facing hardship,
there is now a natural ceiling on how many genuinely charitable
energy-specific projects
exist within our district.
As a result, annual grant levels have remained largely static until very
recently, and the trust capital has continued to grow.
Our ability to respond to broader community need has been artificially
restricted. The issue we face is not a lack of demand, but
eligible scope for organizations to apply for funding.
The Eastern Bay of Plenty is highly deprived, and the community need is obvious.
Trustees are doing what they can, with the vast majority of applications we receive
being funded.
Yet when we look across to our peers and neighboring communities, we know that we
can be doing much more. The Eastern Bay of Plenty includes some of the most
deprived communities in the country, and we all know that
community wellbeing extends well beyond energy purposes alone
into areas such as housing, health, education, the
environment, and social cohesion.
By comparison, neighboring charitable trusts with broader purposes have been able
to deliver significantly greater per person funding over the same
period.
The trustees believe, strongly and unanimously, that the trust has the
capacity, and indeed the obligation, to do more.
The bill proposes making two targeted and principled changes.
Firstly, it broadens the trust purposes from being
limited to energy-related purposes to supporting any charitable
purpose recognized in New Zealand law,
which allows us to continue to fund energy-related initiatives as well as
other activities.
This does not alter our charitable status or allow private
benefit
or change how or in what we invest funds to generate returns for
our granting.
And importantly, it does not diminish our energy-related
heritage.It simply allows trustees to respond
appropriately to real and evolving needs of our communities.
Secondly, the bill introduces a sensible mechanism for future trust deed
updates.
Rather than requiring another private act of Parliament, any future changes would
be assessed by trustees as being in the trust's best interests,
further its charitable purposes, and require High Court approval.
This provides flexibility while maintaining strong and independent safeguards.
Why do we need to come to Parliament to do this?
Over the trust's history, it has explored every available alternative and
found no other way to achieve this change.
The courts cannot make the change under existing trust law, and the Charitable
Trust Act expressly prevents the type of purpose expansion
proposed here.
Only Parliament has the authority to modernize the trust's purposes
in this way.
Importantly, had the trust been set up slightly differently in '94, these
broader purposes could have existed from the outset, as they
do for many comparable trusts.
This bill seeks to correct this historic anomaly.
The trustees have not taken preparing a bill to Parliament lightly
and have sought community-wide mandate to do so.
Our region-wide community poll showed over 80% support for both
proposed changes, and we have received strong written support from local
councils, iwi and hapū, fellow charitable trusts, community
organizations, and our settler.
This is a well-tested, community-endorsed proposal.
In closing, this bill is not about expanding power.
It is about unlocking public benefit for community good.
It will allow the trust to reset and scale its community funding in proportion
with the equity it holds on behalf of the community.
It modernizes a 30-year-old trust deed while preserving charitable
integrity and strong oversight. Most importantly, it
allows Trust Horizon to better serve the people of the Eastern Bay of Plenty,
today and for generations to come.
We really appreciate the committee's consideration of this matter, and we
respectfully ask the committee to recommend the bill to proceed.

They sure are.
Helen, question.
Sorry, just a final question from me.

improving
the housing stockIn 2018,

just

OMA. That would be a start.

the provision of social housing, it's a
